(born July 24, 1963)[[1]](#cite_note-2) is an American former professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") player in the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA). Nicknamed "**the Mailman**", he spent his first 18 seasons (1985–2003) in the NBA with the [Utah Jazz](/wiki/Utah_Jazz "Utah Jazz") and formed a formidable duo with his teammate [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton"). He was a two-time [NBA Most Valuable Player](/wiki/NBA_Most_Valuable_Player "NBA Most Valuable Player"), a 14-time [NBA All-Star](/wiki/NBA_All-Star "NBA All-Star"), and a 14-time member of the [All-NBA](/wiki/All-NBA "All-NBA") Team, which include 11 consecutive First Team selection. His 36,928 career points scored rank third [all-time in NBA history](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_career_scoring_leaders "List of National Basketball Association career scoring leaders") and he holds the records for [most free throws made](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_career_free_throw_scoring_leaders "List of National Basketball Association career free throw scoring leaders") and attempted, in addition to being tied for the second-most first-team All-NBA selections with [Kobe Bryant](/wiki/Kobe_Bryant "Kobe Bryant"), both behind [LeBron James](/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James").[[2]](#cite_note-3)

**Karl Anthony Malone** (born July 24, 1963)[[1]](#cite_note-2) is an American former professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") player in the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA). Nicknamed "**the Mailman**", he spent his first 18 seasons (1985–2003) in the NBA with the [Utah Jazz](/wiki/Utah_Jazz "Utah Jazz") and formed a formidable duo with his teammate [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton"). He was a two-time [NBA Most Valuable Player](/wiki/NBA_Most_Valuable_Player "NBA Most Valuable Player"), a 14-time [NBA All-Star](/wiki/NBA_All-Star "NBA All-Star"), and a 14-time member of the [All-NBA](/wiki/All-NBA "All-NBA") Team, which include 11 consecutive First Team selection. His 36,928 career points scored rank third [all-time in NBA history](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_career_scoring_leaders "List of National Basketball Association career scoring leaders") and he holds the records for [most free throws made](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_career_free_throw_scoring_leaders "List of National Basketball Association career free throw scoring leaders") and attempted, in addition to being tied for the second-most first-team All-NBA selections with [Kobe Bryant](/wiki/Kobe_Bryant "Kobe Bryant"), both behind [LeBron James](/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James").[[2]](#cite_note-3)

Malone played [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") for the [Louisiana Tech Bulldogs](/wiki/Louisiana_Tech_Bulldogs_basketball "Louisiana Tech Bulldogs basketball"). In his three seasons, he helped the Bulldogs to their first [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship") in 1984 and to first place in the [Southland Conference](/wiki/Southland_Conference "Southland Conference") in 1985. The Utah Jazz selected him in the first round of the [1985 NBA draft](/wiki/1985_NBA_draft "1985 NBA draft") with the 13th overall pick. Malone appeared in the playoffs every season in his career, including the [NBA Finals](/wiki/NBA_Finals "NBA Finals") in [1997](/wiki/1997_NBA_Finals "1997 NBA Finals") and [1998](/wiki/1998_NBA_Finals "1998 NBA Finals") with the Jazz. He played his final season with the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers"), with whom he played his third Finals in [2004](/wiki/2004_NBA_Finals "2004 NBA Finals"). Malone also competed with the [United States national team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_basketball_team "United States men's national basketball team") in the [Summer Olympic Games of 1992](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1992_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1992 Summer Olympics") and [1996](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1996_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1996 Summer Olympics"); in both years he won gold medals. In 1996, as part of the NBA's 50th Anniversary, Malone was honored as one of the 50 greatest players of all time by being named to the [NBA 50th Anniversary Team](/wiki/50_Greatest_Players_in_NBA_History "50 Greatest Players in NBA History").[[3]](#cite_note-4) In October 2021, Malone was again honored as one of the league's greatest players of all time by being named to the [NBA 75th Anniversary Team](/wiki/NBA_75th_Anniversary_Team "NBA 75th Anniversary Team").[[4]](#cite_note-5)

After retiring from the NBA, Malone joined the staff of the Louisiana Tech Bulldogs in 2007 and was inducted into the [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") in 2010 twice – for his individual career, and as a member of the [1992 United States men's Olympic basketball team](/wiki/1992_United_States_men%27s_Olympic_basketball_team "1992 United States men's Olympic basketball team").[[5]](#cite_note-6) Malone is often considered one of the greatest [power forwards](/wiki/Power_forward "Power forward") in NBA history,[[6]](#cite_note-Britannica-7) but his personal conduct has attracted criticism following revelations that, while attending college at Louisiana Tech, he impregnated a 13-year-old.[[7]](#cite_note-8) No statutory rape charges were filed.[[8]](#cite_note-vr2wx-9)[[9]](#cite_note-:1-10)

## Early life

Born in [Summerfield, Louisiana](/wiki/Summerfield,_Louisiana "Summerfield, Louisiana"),[[6]](#cite_note-Britannica-7) Malone was the youngest of nine children and was raised on a farm by his single mother, Shirley. Shedrick Hay, his father, was married to a different woman and raising a family with her. Shedrick committed suicide when Karl was 14,[[10]](#cite_note-11) though Malone didn't disclose the suicide until 1994. As a child, Malone often worked on the farm, chopped trees, hunted, and fished. He attended Summerfield High School and led his basketball team to three consecutive Louisiana Class C titles from 1979 to his senior season in 1981.

## College career

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Karl_Malone_with_Louisiana_Tech%2C_January_1982.jpg/250px-Karl_Malone_with_Louisiana_Tech%2C_January_1982.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Malone_with_Louisiana_Tech,_January_1982.jpg)

Malone with the [Louisiana Tech Bulldogs](/wiki/Louisiana_Tech_Bulldogs_basketball "Louisiana Tech Bulldogs basketball") in 1982

Malone was recruited by [University of Arkansas basketball](/wiki/Arkansas_Razorbacks_men%27s_basketball "Arkansas Razorbacks men's basketball") coach [Eddie Sutton](/wiki/Eddie_Sutton "Eddie Sutton") and [Louisiana Tech basketball](/wiki/Louisiana_Tech_Bulldogs_basketball "Louisiana Tech Bulldogs basketball") coach [Andy Russo](/wiki/Andy_Russo "Andy Russo"). Originally opting for Arkansas, Malone later weighed the option to sign with Louisiana Tech, which was closer to home. This was partially influenced by his close friend and teammate [Benny Anders](/wiki/Benny_Anders "Benny Anders"), who had also been recruited by Arkansas, signing with the [University of Houston](/wiki/University_of_Houston "University of Houston") instead. Malone ultimately signed with Louisiana Tech in April 1981.[[11]](#cite_note-12) However, he was ineligible to join the [Louisiana Tech Bulldogs basketball](/wiki/Louisiana_Tech_Bulldogs_basketball "Louisiana Tech Bulldogs basketball") team because his grades were too low for freshman eligibility.[[12]](#cite_note-ESPN_Classic-13) By the end of his freshman year, he had pushed his GPA up to 2.4, making him eligible for the basketball team the following year.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

In his first season with Louisiana Tech (1982–83), Malone became the first person in the [Southland Conference](/wiki/Southland_Conference "Southland Conference") to be named Outstanding Player and Outstanding Newcomer in the same year.[[14]](#cite_note-15) In 1983, he competed in that year's [Summer Universiade](/wiki/1983_Summer_Universiade "1983 Summer Universiade") along with fellow future NBA player [Charles Barkley](/wiki/Charles_Barkley "Charles Barkley").[[15]](#cite_note-16) In his second season (1983–84), Malone averaged 18.7 points and 9.3 rebounds per game.[[12]](#cite_note-ESPN_Classic-13)[[16]](#cite_note-basketball-reference-17) Louisiana Tech finished the 1984–85 season 29–3, at the top of the [Southland Conference](/wiki/Southland_Conference "Southland Conference"), and advanced to the NCAA tournament for the first time in school history; the team finished at the [Sweet 16 round](/wiki/1985_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament#Midwest_region "1985 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament").[[17]](#cite_note-18) In each of his three seasons with the Louisiana Tech Bulldogs, Malone was an All-Southland selection.[[12]](#cite_note-ESPN_Classic-13)

## Professional career

### Utah Jazz (1985–2003)

#### Early years (1985–1987)

In the [1985 NBA draft](/wiki/1985_NBA_draft "1985 NBA draft"), the [Utah Jazz](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Utah_Jazz_season "1985–86 Utah Jazz season") selected Karl Malone with the 13th overall pick. According to Malone's official NBA biography: "If professional scouts had correctly predicted the impact Karl Malone would have on the NBA, Malone would have been picked much higher than 13th in the 1985 NBA Draft."[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) In fact, Malone was so convinced the [Dallas Mavericks](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Dallas_Mavericks_season "1985–86 Dallas Mavericks season") were going to select him with the eighth choice that he had already rented an apartment in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"). Instead, the Mavericks selected [Detlef Schrempf](/wiki/Detlef_Schrempf "Detlef Schrempf").[[19]](#cite_note-20) Under head coach [Frank Layden](/wiki/Frank_Layden "Frank Layden"), Malone averaged 14.9 points and 8.9 rebounds in his first season and made the 1986 [NBA All-Rookie Team](/wiki/NBA_All-Rookie_Team "NBA All-Rookie Team") after coming in third for [Rookie of the Year](/wiki/NBA_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "NBA Rookie of the Year Award") votes.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) On January 14, 1986, the Jazz beat the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Houston_Rockets_season "1985–86 Houston Rockets season") 105–102 to snap the Rockets' 20-game winning streak at home. Malone scored 29 points in that game, including four free throws followed by a three-pointer by [Pace Mannion](/wiki/Pace_Mannion "Pace Mannion") to rally from a 96–89 deficit with 5 minutes and 36 seconds remaining to a 96–96 tie.[[20]](#cite_note-21) For the third consecutive season, the Jazz made the postseason but lost the first round of the [1986 playoffs](/wiki/1986_NBA_Playoffs "1986 NBA Playoffs") to the Dallas Mavericks. In the four playoff games, Malone improved in his scoring with a 20 points per game average but was still subpar in shooting (49.6% field goals) and rebounds (8.9). After his second season, Malone became the Jazz's leader in average scoring (21.7 points) and rebounding (10.4 rebounds); in 24 of 29 games between February 1 and April 3, 1987, he was the leading scorer of the game.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19)

#### All-Star and team offense leader (1987–1996)

By the [1987–88 season](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_NBA_season "1987–88 NBA season"), Malone was the foundation of the offense and [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton") was the floor general. Malone made his first All-Star Game in 1988 on the strength of 27.1 points per game, and made his first All-NBA team at the end of the season. This was the first of 14 consecutive All-Star appearances for Malone.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) In the [1988 NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/1988_NBA_All-Star_Game "1988 NBA All-Star Game"), Malone led the Western Conference All-Star team with 22 points.[[21]](#cite_note-NYT_1988-22) The Jazz finished 47–35, third place in the Midwest Division, and defeated the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_Portland_Trail_Blazers_season "1987–88 Portland Trail Blazers season") in the first round.[[22]](#cite_note-23) In the next round, the defending champions [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_Los_Angeles_Lakers_season "1987–88 Los Angeles Lakers season"), led by perennial All-Stars [Magic Johnson](/wiki/Magic_Johnson "Magic Johnson"), [James Worthy](/wiki/James_Worthy "James Worthy"), and [Kareem Abdul-Jabbar](/wiki/Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar "Kareem Abdul-Jabbar"), defeated the Jazz in seven games. In the seventh game of the series, Malone scored 31 points and had 15 rebounds, but the Lakers beat the Jazz 109–98 and eventually won the [1988 NBA Finals](/wiki/1988_NBA_Finals "1988 NBA Finals"). In 11 playoff games in 1988, Malone averaged 29.7 points and 11.8 rebounds.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Karl_Malone_1988-89.jpg/250px-Karl_Malone_1988-89.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Malone_1988-89.jpg)

Malone circa 1988

Malone signed a 10-year contract during the 1988 offseason worth $18 million.[[21]](#cite_note-NYT_1988-22) In December 1988, [Jerry Sloan](/wiki/Jerry_Sloan "Jerry Sloan") succeeded Layden as head coach as Layden became team president.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Malone averaged 29.1 points in [1988–89](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_NBA_season "1988–89 NBA season"), good for second in the NBA behind [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan"), and 10.7 rebounds, which was fifth in the league.[[24]](#cite_note-25) This scoring average was Malone's highest so far in his career. At the [1989 NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/1989_NBA_All-Star_Game "1989 NBA All-Star Game"), Malone finished with 28 points, 9 rebounds and 3 assists en route to his first [NBA All-Star MVP](/wiki/NBA_All-Star_MVP "NBA All-Star MVP"). The Jazz finished 51–31, but were swept in three games in the first round by the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_Golden_State_Warriors_season "1988–89 Golden State Warriors season"). This season marked Malone's first with the [All-NBA First Team](/wiki/All-NBA_First_Team "All-NBA First Team") honor.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19)

During the [1989–90](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_NBA_season "1989–90 NBA season"), Malone increased his scoring to 31 points and his rebounding to 11.1 a game and again was selected to the All-NBA First Team.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) In the first game of the season, Malone recorded 40 points, 16 rebounds and 5 assists in a 122–113 win over the Denver Nuggets.[[25]](#cite_note-26) On December 5, 1989, Malone scored 21 points and grabbed 19 rebounds in a 94–80 win over the home team, Cleveland Cavaliers.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Seventeen days later, Malone recorded 52 points and 17 rebounds in a 114–100 road win over the Charlotte Hornets.[[27]](#cite_note-28) On January 27, 1990, Malone scored a career-high 61 points in a 144–96 victory against the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks").[[28]](#cite_note-29) He made 21 of 26 [field goals](/wiki/Field_goal_(basketball) "Field goal (basketball)") and 19 of 23 free throws.[[29]](#cite_note-SLT_Hall-30) It was the most points scored by a Jazz player since the team moved to Utah from New Orleans. Although Malone was voted to the NBA All-Star Game for the third consecutive season, because of an ankle injury he sat out the game.[[30]](#cite_note-31) He led the team in scoring in 24 of the last 26 games of the season; on March 29, 1990, against the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_Golden_State_Warriors_season "1989–90 Golden State Warriors season"), Malone scored 49 points, and on April 12 against the [Lakers](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_Los_Angeles_Lakers_season "1989–90 Los Angeles Lakers season") he scored 45. The Jazz, finishing the season 55–27, lost to the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_Phoenix_Suns_season "1989–90 Phoenix Suns season") within five games in the first round of the [playoffs](/wiki/1990_NBA_Playoffs "1990 NBA Playoffs"), in which Malone averaged 25.2 points and 10.2 rebounds. For the second straight season, Malone finished second in the league in points per game behind Michael Jordan.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19)

From January 19 to March 4, 1991, Malone led the Jazz in scoring for 19 straight games; after starting the [1990–91](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_NBA_season "1990–91 NBA season") season 7–8 the team went 21–9 in January and February 1991. Malone scored 16 points and took 11 rebounds in the [1991 NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/1991_NBA_All-Star_Game "1991 NBA All-Star Game"), his fourth consecutive All-Star appearance for the West, and averaged 29.0 points and 11.8 rebounds each regular-season game. He was among four Jazz players with double-figure scoring averages, the others being the newly acquired [Jeff Malone](/wiki/Jeff_Malone "Jeff Malone") (no relation) as well as John Stockton and [Thurl Bailey](/wiki/Thurl_Bailey "Thurl Bailey"). In four games, the Jazz eliminated the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_Phoenix_Suns_season "1990–91 Phoenix Suns season") in the first round of the [1991 playoffs](/wiki/1991_NBA_Playoffs "1991 NBA Playoffs") but lost to the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_Portland_Trail_Blazers_season "1990–91 Portland Trail Blazers season") in the second round. Malone made the All-NBA First Team for the third consecutive season.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19)

As he had done in 1989–90, Malone finished the 1991–92 season second in the league in scoring, averaging 28.0 points per game. He made the All-NBA First Team for the fourth straight year.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) It was also a breakout season for the Jazz; during the [1992 NBA Playoffs](/wiki/1992_NBA_Playoffs "1992 NBA Playoffs"), the team made the Western Conference Finals for the first time in franchise history.[[31]](#cite_note-Utah_index-32) Malone scored 40 or more points in five games this season.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) Despite his continued success and achievements, Malone encountered trouble for committing a [flagrant foul](/wiki/Flagrant_foul "Flagrant foul"). On December 14, 1991, when the Jazz played against the [Detroit Pistons](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Detroit_Pistons_season "1991–92 Detroit Pistons season"), Malone elbowed the Pistons' [Isiah Thomas](/wiki/Isiah_Thomas "Isiah Thomas") on the forehead. Thomas needed 40 stitches over his eye, and the NBA suspended Malone for the next game without pay and fined him $10,000.[[32]](#cite_note-33) In its first-ever franchise Western Conference Finals appearance, in six games the Jazz lost in the playoffs for the second straight season to the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Portland_Trail_Blazers_season "1991–92 Portland Trail Blazers season"). Malone averaged 29.1 points on 52.1% shooting and 11.9 rebounds in the 1992 playoffs.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19)

Throughout the 1990s, Malone put up stellar numbers: in [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_NBA_season "1992–93 NBA season"), he averaged 27.0 points and 11.2 rebounds per game, 25.2 points and 11.5 rebounds in [1993–94](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_NBA_season "1993–94 NBA season"), 26.7 points and 10.6 rebounds in [1994–95](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NBA_season "1994–95 NBA season"), and 25.7 points and 9.8 rebounds in [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_NBA_season "1995–96 NBA season").[[33]](#cite_note-NBA_playerfile-34) Following the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1992_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1992 Summer Olympics") in which Malone helped the [American national team](/wiki/1992_United_States_men%27s_Olympic_basketball_team "1992 United States men's Olympic basketball team"), dubbed the "Dream Team", win a gold medal, Malone expressed opposition to [Magic Johnson](/wiki/Magic_Johnson "Magic Johnson"), who had recently tested positive for [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV") and retired from the NBA in 1991, making a comeback to the league. Malone's point of view diverged from the support for Johnson from his Olympic and [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") teammates, and the NBA implemented [AIDS](/wiki/AIDS "AIDS")-related precautions after Johnson's revelation.[[34]](#cite_note-35) On February 4, 1993, in a game against the Lakers, Malone surpassed 16,000 career points. He and his teammate John Stockton shared the [All-Star MVP](/wiki/NBA_All-Star_Game_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "NBA All-Star Game Most Valuable Player Award") award in [1993](/wiki/1993_NBA_All-Star_Game "1993 NBA All-Star Game"). In the West's 135–132 overtime win, Malone scored 28 points and pulled in 10 rebounds.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19)

Malone started all 82 games of 1993–94 and helped the Jazz make the Western Conference Finals for the second time in franchise history and his career. In his ninth season, Malone led the Jazz in scoring (25.2), rebounding (11.5), and blocked shots (126), made 49.7% of field goal attempts, and played 3,329 minutes, the second-most that season in the league behind [Latrell Sprewell](/wiki/Latrell_Sprewell "Latrell Sprewell")'s 3,533.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) On March 29, 1994, Malone recorded a career-high 23 rebounds, but the Jazz lost to the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Golden_State_Warriors_season "1993–94 Golden State Warriors season") 116–113. However, Malone made only 8 of 29 field goals that night and commented post-game: "My rebounds are not going to be tomorrow's headline...Tomorrow's headline is going to be all those easy shots I missed."[[35]](#cite_note-36) The eventual [NBA champion](/wiki/1994_NBA_Finals "1994 NBA Finals") [Houston Rockets](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Houston_Rockets_season "1993–94 Houston Rockets season") eliminated the Jazz from the Western Conference Finals in five games.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) Although Malone scored 32 points in the Jazz' 104–99 Game 2 loss, along with Stockton's 18, they and the Jazz were no match for the Rockets with high-scoring center [Hakeem Olajuwon](/wiki/Hakeem_Olajuwon "Hakeem Olajuwon"). Midway into the fourth quarter, Malone and Olajuwon scored on four consecutive possessions by their teams, leading up to a 93–93 tie.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

For the first time in team history, in 1994–95, the Utah Jazz won 60 games. In addition, the team won 15 consecutive away games (the best such streak by the team, then the second-best streak in the league). Malone's 26.7 points per game ranked fourth in the NBA, and Malone became the 19th NBA player to reach 20,000 career points on January 20, 1995. In the [1995 playoffs](/wiki/1995_NBA_Playoffs "1995 NBA Playoffs"), the Jazz lost to the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Houston_Rockets_season "1994–95 Houston Rockets season") for the second consecutive year, this time in the first round.[[18]](#cite_note-NBA_bio-19) The Rockets became [NBA champions for the second straight season](/wiki/1995_NBA_Finals "1995 NBA Finals").

On January 13, 1996, Malone renewed his contract with the Jazz.[[33]](#cite_note-NBA_playerfile-34) The Jazz only made it as far as the Western Conference Finals in this period, losing to the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Portland_Trail_Blazers_season "1991–92 Portland Trail Blazers season") (1992), the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Houston_Rockets_season "1993–94 Houston Rockets season") (1994) and the [Seattle SuperSonics](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Seattle_SuperSonics_season "1995–96 Seattle SuperSonics season") (1996).

#### Western Conference championship years (1996–1998)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3e/Lipofsky-Karl-Malone-32727.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lipofsky-Karl-Malone-32727.jpg)

Malone in 1997

Malone returned from a [gold medal winning-effort at the 1996 Summer Olympics](#Olympic_career) leading the Jazz to two consecutive NBA Finals appearances. During the 1996–97 season, Malone put up a resurgent 27.4 points per game while leading the Jazz to a 64–18 record, the most regular-season wins in team history. Malone won his first [NBA Most Valuable Player](/wiki/NBA_Most_Valuable_Player "NBA Most Valuable Player") award, and the Jazz were the top team in the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(NBA) "Western Conference (NBA)") and the playoff champions in that conference.[[12]](#cite_note-ESPN_Classic-13)[[37]](#cite_note-38) After sweeping the [Los Angeles Clippers](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Los_Angeles_Clippers_season "1996–97 Los Angeles Clippers season") and defeating the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Los_Angeles_Lakers_season "1996–97 Los Angeles Lakers season"), the Jazz took on the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Houston_Rockets_season "1996–97 Houston Rockets season"), led by the aging trio of [Hakeem Olajuwon](/wiki/Hakeem_Olajuwon "Hakeem Olajuwon"), [Charles Barkley](/wiki/Charles_Barkley "Charles Barkley"), and [Clyde Drexler](/wiki/Clyde_Drexler "Clyde Drexler"). The Jazz beat them in six games (the last victory coming on a last-second shot by Stockton). Malone finally reached the NBA Finals in [1997](/wiki/1997_NBA_Finals "1997 NBA Finals"), where they played the [Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan")-led [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Chicago_Bulls_season "1996–97 Chicago Bulls season"). In a matchup of the two previous MVPs, the Bulls won the first two games at the [United Center](/wiki/United_Center "United Center"). Malone struggled from the field, going 6 of 20 for 20 points in Game 2. However, the Jazz won the next two games at the [Delta Center](/wiki/Delta_Center "Delta Center") behind Malone's 37 points in Game 3 and 23 in Game 4, including a game-winning fastbreak lay-up off a spectacular assist by Stockton in the last minute. The Bulls won the next two games and the series, with Malone struggling from the foul line in Game 6.

The next season saw the Jazz once again dominate. Malone put up 27 points per game and just missed out on his second MVP award, losing to Jordan. Nevertheless, the Jazz posted a 62–20 record, which was the best in the NBA. The Jazz once again were seated at the top of the Western Conference, and in the 1998 playoffs they defeated the [Rockets](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Houston_Rockets_season "1997–98 Houston Rockets season"), [Spurs](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_San_Antonio_Spurs_season "1997–98 San Antonio Spurs season"), and [Lakers](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Los_Angeles_Lakers_season "1997–98 Los Angeles Lakers season") via a sweep en route to their second consecutive [Finals](/wiki/1998_NBA_Finals "1998 NBA Finals") appearance. The rematch with the [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Chicago_Bulls_season "1997–98 Chicago Bulls season") would start differently, as Malone put up 21 points and the Jazz won Game 1, 88–85. Malone found himself unable to put up consistently high numbers, due in large part to the swarming defense of defenders [Dennis Rodman](/wiki/Dennis_Rodman "Dennis Rodman") and [Scottie Pippen](/wiki/Scottie_Pippen "Scottie Pippen"). In Game 5 of the 1998 Finals, Malone led the Jazz in scoring with 39 points, and the Jazz beat the Bulls 83–81 in Chicago. Malone scored his 39 points on 17-for-27 shooting and also made 9 rebounds, 5 assists, and 1 turnover. Many of his shots were "mid-range turnaround jumpers from the left side".[[38]](#cite_note-1998_game_5_ESPN-39)

A [sixth game](/wiki/Game_6_of_the_1998_NBA_Finals "Game 6 of the 1998 NBA Finals") of the Finals was held at the Delta Center in Salt Lake City, and by trailing the series 3–2 the team was one loss away from losing the series.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Malone scored 31 points and made 11 rebounds. Although the Jazz held leads of 49–45 by halftime and 66–61 after the third quarter, the team squandered their lead in the fourth and would lose after Malone lost a pass in the [post](/wiki/Low_post "Low post").[[40]](#cite_note-41) With 18.9 seconds left in the fourth quarter and the Jazz leading 86–85, Bulls guard/forward [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") stole the ball just passed to Malone and with 5.2 seconds left made a jump shot that gave the Bulls an 87–86 lead. [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton") missed a game-winning jump shot. Jordan's shot has been dubbed the "greatest shot" of his career, and the Bulls' 1998 title was their third consecutive championship and sixth since 1991.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

#### Later seasons with Jazz (1998–2003)

On November 12, 1998, during the [lockout that shortened the 1998–99 season](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_NBA_lockout "1998–99 NBA lockout"), Malone declared on his Los Angeles-based radio show that he would demand a trade from the Jazz after the end of the lockout, citing a lack of respect from the team owner [Larry H. Miller](/wiki/Larry_H._Miller "Larry H. Miller") and the Utah media, which he dubbed "goons".[[42]](#cite_note-43) A week later, Malone backed off on his trade demands, stating in an interview that he would still love to play for the Jazz with "everything else being equal".[[43]](#cite_note-44) The NBA regular season resumed in February and Malone won his second MVP award, with the Jazz going 37–13 during the 50-game regular season. They lost in the second round that year to the [Trail Blazers](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Portland_Trail_Blazers_season "1998–99 Portland Trail Blazers season"), and for the next several years the Jazz fell out of title contention. The Jazz won Game 5 of the second-round [1999](/wiki/1999_NBA_Playoffs "1999 NBA Playoffs") round against the Blazers 88–71 to force a sixth game, and Malone scored 23 points. In that game, Malone elbowed the Blazers' [Brian Grant](/wiki/Brian_Grant "Brian Grant"), something he also did in Game 1 and for which he was fined $10,000.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Despite the decline of his team and his advancing age, Malone averaged 25.5, 23.2, 22.4, and 20.6 points per game in his last four seasons with Utah. He became a free agent in 2003, the year Stockton had retired. For the time Malone and Stockton played together on the Jazz (1985–2003), the two formed one of the most productive guard–forward combinations in NBA history.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Playing coach [Jerry Sloan](/wiki/Jerry_Sloan "Jerry Sloan")'s scrappy and tough style and perfecting the [pick and roll](/wiki/Pick_and_roll "Pick and roll") to a maximum degree of efficiency, the Jazz regularly made the playoffs with a winning record. Malone led the Jazz to multiple 50-win seasons with the exception of [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_NBA_season "1992–93 NBA season") (47–35).

### Los Angeles Lakers (2003–2004)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/31/Karl_Malone_Lakers_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Malone_Lakers_(cropped).jpg)

Malone with the Lakers

Malone played in the NBA for one more season, joining the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Los_Angeles_Lakers_season "2003–04 Los Angeles Lakers season") in an attempt to win a championship. All-Star point guard [Gary Payton](/wiki/Gary_Payton "Gary Payton") also joined the Lakers in an attempt to win his first NBA championship, and with Malone, Payton, [Shaquille O'Neal](/wiki/Shaquille_O%27Neal "Shaquille O'Neal") and [Kobe Bryant](/wiki/Kobe_Bryant "Kobe Bryant"), the Lakers were favorites to win the title. The No. 32 jersey Malone had worn for 19 years with the Jazz was already retired in honor of [Magic Johnson](/wiki/Magic_Johnson "Magic Johnson"), and while Johnson offered to allow Malone to wear the number, Malone ultimately declined,[[45]](#cite_note-46) and instead wore a number 11 jersey.[[46]](#cite_note-47) The Lakers rolled out to a dominant 18–3 start to the season, but on December 21, Malone suffered a knee injury against the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns")[[47]](#cite_note-48) that forced him to miss 39 games. Malone returned by the season's end, as the Lakers entered the playoffs with 56 wins and the number 2 seed in the playoffs. In the first round against the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets"), Malone posted double-figure scoring in 4 games, including a 30-point and 13-rebound effort in the Lakers' game 4 victory. The Lakers advanced to face the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") in the conference semifinals, where Malone played a critical role on defense against [Tim Duncan](/wiki/Tim_Duncan "Tim Duncan"), despite not scoring more than 13 points in any game during the series. The Lakers defeated the Spurs in 6 games in the conference semifinals before defeating the [Minnesota Timberwolves](/wiki/Minnesota_Timberwolves "Minnesota Timberwolves") in 6 games in the conference finals to advance to the NBA Finals. He scored 17 points on two occasions against Minnesota and averaged 11.5 points and 8.8 rebounds a game in the playoffs.

The 40-year-old Malone's quest to win an NBA championship failed as the Lakers were defeated in five games by the [Detroit Pistons](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Detroit_Pistons_season "2003–04 Detroit Pistons season") in the [2004 NBA Finals](/wiki/2004_NBA_Finals "2004 NBA Finals"). Malone sprained his right knee in game three and played injured in games three and four before missing the fifth and final game of the series.

### Retirement

After his season with the Lakers, Malone became a [free agent](/wiki/Free_agent "Free agent"). He had knee surgery during the summer of 2004, and personal problems[[48]](#cite_note-49) with Lakers guard [Kobe Bryant](/wiki/Kobe_Bryant "Kobe Bryant"),[[49]](#cite_note-50) who prompted Malone not to return for another season with the team. The [New York Knicks](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_New_York_Knicks_season "2004–05 New York Knicks season") sought to sign Malone for 2004–05.[[50]](#cite_note-51) In early February 2005, prior to the [2005 NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/2005_NBA_All-Star_Game "2005 NBA All-Star Game"), Malone's agent speculated that Malone would sign with the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs").[[51]](#cite_note-52) Spurs head coach [Gregg Popovich](/wiki/Gregg_Popovich "Gregg Popovich") confirmed his team was interested in signing Malone, should he decide to play one more season.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

At a press conference on February 13, 2005, at the Delta Center, Malone officially announced his retirement from the NBA after 19 seasons.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

The Utah Jazz retired Malone's jersey number 32 on March 23, 2006, when the Jazz hosted the [Washington Wizards](/wiki/Washington_Wizards "Washington Wizards"). He was also honored with the unveiling of a [bronze statue](/wiki/Bronze_sculpture "Bronze sculpture") outside the [EnergySolutions Arena](/wiki/Vivint_Smart_Home_Arena "Vivint Smart Home Arena") next to teammate [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton"), and the renaming of a portion of 100 South in [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City") in his honor. The location where the statues stand is now the intersection of Stockton and Malone.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

## Olympic career

In 1984, Malone and Stockton were both cut from the [United States men's national basketball team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_basketball_team "United States men's national basketball team") but joined the team in 1992, when national basketball teams welcomed NBA players. Prior to that only professionals from leagues other than the NBA were allowed to compete, and that greatly disadvantaged the United States.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

The [Oregon National Guard](/wiki/Oregon_National_Guard "Oregon National Guard") made Malone an honorary member after the U.S. national team beat the [Venezuelan team](/wiki/Venezuela_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Venezuela men's national basketball team") 127–80 in the gold-medal game of the 1992 Olympic qualifier tournament.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Malone played in the [1992](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics") and [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics") and won gold medals with the US team both years. In the 1996 Olympics, Malone averaged 8.4 points, 4.5 rebounds, and 1.4 assists per game and made 56.9% of two-point field goal attempts and 52.9% of free-throw attempts.[[57]](#cite_note-FIBA_1996-58) In 2010, he was inducted into Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame along with the rest of the original [Dream Team](/wiki/1992_United_States_men%27s_Olympic_basketball_team "1992 United States men's Olympic basketball team").[[58]](#cite_note-59)

## Coaching career

### Louisiana Tech (2007–2011)

On May 31, 2007, Malone became director of basketball promotion and assistant strength and conditioning coach at his alma mater [Louisiana Tech University](/wiki/Louisiana_Tech_University "Louisiana Tech University") and donated $350,000 to the university athletic department.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

On May 29, 2013, Malone returned to the Utah Jazz to work as a big man coach.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

## Player profile

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/SLC_KarlMaloneStatue_04Oct2017.jpg/250px-SLC_KarlMaloneStatue_04Oct2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:SLC_KarlMaloneStatue_04Oct2017.jpg)

Malone's statue outside the [Delta Center](/wiki/Delta_Center "Delta Center") (then Vivint Smart Home Arena, pictured in 2017)

Malone is regarded as one of the best power forwards in the history of the NBA and was known as "the Mailman" for his consistent delivery.[[21]](#cite_note-NYT_1988-22) He collected two regular-season MVP Awards, eleven NBA First Team nominations and was also selected to the NBA All-Defensive First Team three times. In 2022, to commemorate the NBA's 75th Anniversary *[The Athletic](/wiki/The_Athletic "The Athletic")* ranked their top 75 players of all time, and named Malone as the 16th greatest player in NBA history.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

Malone wore number 32 for the Utah Jazz. He wore [number 11](/wiki/11_(number) "11 (number)") for the Los Angeles Lakers (number 32 was retired honoring Magic Johnson, though Johnson himself offered to have it unretired for Malone to wear, an offer Malone declined), though he was photographed with a number 32 jersey at his Lakers introductory press conference. Malone also wore number 11 for the Dream Team, as the players wore 4 to 15 to adhere to [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA") rules.

Malone has also played 193 playoff games without winning an [NBA championship](/wiki/List_of_NBA_champions "List of NBA champions"), the most in playoff history.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

## Other ventures

### Businesses

Malone is the owner of Teriyaki Grill, Eskamoe's Frozen Custard & More, and [Arby's](/wiki/Arby%27s "Arby's") in [Ruston, Louisiana](/wiki/Ruston,_Louisiana "Ruston, Louisiana").[[63]](#cite_note-:0-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65)

He also owns two car dealerships in Utah and one in Louisiana. Karl Malone Toyota is in the Salt Lake City suburb of [Draper, Utah](/wiki/Draper,_Utah "Draper, Utah"), while Karl Malone Chrysler Dodge Jeep Ram is in [Heber City, Utah](/wiki/Heber_City,_Utah "Heber City, Utah"). Malone previously co-owned a Toyota dealership in [Albuquerque, New Mexico](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico"), with [Larry H. Miller Dealerships](/wiki/Larry_H._Miller_Dealerships "Larry H. Miller Dealerships"), but sold his share in 2010. He also co-owned a Honda dealership in [Sandy, Utah](/wiki/Sandy,_Utah "Sandy, Utah"), with [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton"), but sold his share, again to Larry H. Miller Dealerships, in 2010. In 2019, he bought a Toyota dealership in [Ruston, Louisiana](/wiki/Ruston,_Louisiana "Ruston, Louisiana"), now called Karl Malone Toyota of Ruston.

Malone also owns three [Jiffy Lube](/wiki/Jiffy_Lube "Jiffy Lube") franchises in Utah, and is a part owner of [Burger King franchises](/wiki/Burger_King_franchises "Burger King franchises") in Utah and Idaho.[[63]](#cite_note-:0-64)

### Acting

Malone starred in the 1994 film *Rockwell* as a member of [Porter Rockwell](/wiki/Porter_Rockwell "Porter Rockwell")'s team.[[65]](#cite_note-66) He had a guest appearance as himself and as a superhero named "Pulverizer" in the *[Static Shock](/wiki/Static_Shock "Static Shock")* episode "Hoop Squad".[[66]](#cite_note-67) Malone also made a cameo appearance as himself in the 2004 comedy film *[Soul Plane](/wiki/Soul_Plane "Soul Plane")* and appears in an episode of *[Brother's Keeper](/wiki/Brother%27s_Keeper_(1998_TV_series) "Brother's Keeper (1998 TV series)")* from 1998.[[67]](#cite_note-68) He also has a recurring role in a skit called "All Alone with Karl Malone" on the television series *[Jimmy Kimmel Live!](/wiki/Jimmy_Kimmel_Live! "Jimmy Kimmel Live!")*.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Professional wrestling

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/DDP_KarlMalone_Mail0021.jpg/250px-DDP_KarlMalone_Mail0021.jpg)](/wiki/File:DDP_KarlMalone_Mail0021.jpg)

Malone with [Diamond Dallas Page](/wiki/Diamond_Dallas_Page "Diamond Dallas Page") in 1998

After the [1998 NBA Finals](/wiki/1998_NBA_Finals "1998 NBA Finals"), Malone and Chicago Bulls power forward [Dennis Rodman](/wiki/Dennis_Rodman "Dennis Rodman") participated in a [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") match in [World Championship Wrestling](/wiki/World_Championship_Wrestling "World Championship Wrestling") (WCW) at their [Bash at the Beach](/wiki/Bash_at_the_Beach_1998 "Bash at the Beach 1998") event in July 1998. Malone, a wrestling fan, teamed with his friend, [Diamond Dallas Page](/wiki/Diamond_Dallas_Page "Diamond Dallas Page"), against Rodman and [Hulk Hogan](/wiki/Hulk_Hogan "Hulk Hogan").[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71) In a poorly received match by some wrestling critics, the two power forwards exchanged "rudimentary headlocks, slams and clotheslines" for 23 minutes.[[71]](#cite_note-72) Malone and DDP lost to Rodman and Hogan. The event was a massive financial success for the company, drawing the highest pay-per-view buyrate of 1998 and one of the highest in the company's history.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

## Personal life

### Family

* By Bonita Ford
  + [Cheryl](/wiki/Cheryl_Ford "Cheryl Ford") (born 1981)
  + Daryl (born 1981)
* By Gloria Bell
  + [Demetress](/wiki/Demetress_Bell "Demetress Bell") (born 1984)
* By Kay Kinsey
  + Kadee (born 1991)

  + Karlee (born 1998)

Malone married Kay Kinsey, winner of the 1988 [Miss Idaho USA](/wiki/Miss_Idaho_USA "Miss Idaho USA") pageant, on December 24, 1990.[[73]](#cite_note-Laker_wives-74) They have four children: daughter Kadee (born November 8, 1991),[[74]](#cite_note-75) daughter Kylee (born April 7, 1993), son Karl Jr. "K.J." (born May 8, 1995), and daughter Karlee (born 1998).[[73]](#cite_note-Laker_wives-74) K.J. played football at [LSU](/wiki/LSU_Tigers_football "LSU Tigers football"),[[75]](#cite_note-76) and was invited to the 2018 NFL Combine.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Karl_Malone_in_Afghanistan.jpg/250px-Karl_Malone_in_Afghanistan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Malone_in_Afghanistan.jpg)

Malone in 2011

In 1998, the [tabloid](/wiki/Tabloid_(newspaper_format) "Tabloid (newspaper format)") newspaper *[The Globe](/wiki/The_Globe_(tabloid) "The Globe (tabloid)")* reported that Malone had been a defendant in paternity lawsuits, filed shortly after Malone began his professional basketball career in the late 1980s. The newspaper alleged that he was the father of three children: two by Bonita Ford, a woman approximately his age from his hometown of Summerfield, Louisiana, and one with Gloria Bell, who was 13 when she gave birth. Malone and Ford were both 17 when twins Daryl and [Cheryl Ford](/wiki/Cheryl_Ford "Cheryl Ford") were born;[[77]](#cite_note-78) Malone was a 20-year-old college sophomore when Bell, at age 13, gave birth to [Demetress Bell](/wiki/Demetress_Bell "Demetress Bell").[[9]](#cite_note-:1-10)[[78]](#cite_note-79) Under Louisiana law at the time, a child under the age of 17 could not consent to sexual activities, but no statutory rape charges were filed against Malone.[[8]](#cite_note-vr2wx-9)[[9]](#cite_note-:1-10)[[79]](#cite_note-80) In the aftermath of the *Globe* story, Malone met the Ford twins for the first time since visiting them in the hospital after they were born.[[80]](#cite_note-meets_twins-81) Malone did not meet with Bell at that time, and his attorney insisted that Malone had settled the lawsuits before any conclusive establishment of paternity and thus still did not know whether he was truly the father of any of the children.

The *Tribune* confirmed that the judge in the Bell lawsuit ruled Malone to be the father, not based on presented evidence, but rather because Malone did not respond to the suit. The paper also examined court documents detailing the evidence that was to have been presented had Malone responded and a trial ensued. One item listed was a laboratory blood test that concluded with over 99% certainty that Bell's father was either Malone or a brother of Malone. The paper also reported that applying the same blood sample to the Ford twins resulted in a similarly high probability of paternity by Malone.[[81]](#cite_note-Tribune-82)[[82]](#cite_note-Tribune2-83) According to the *Tribune*, Malone challenged the court's ruling with regard to Bell, claiming that the judgment holding him responsible for $125 per week in child support, plus past and future medical expenses, was excessive. Before Malone's appeal was adjudicated, the lawsuit was settled on confidential terms. In the case of the Ford twins, Malone was ruled to be their father when he violated a court order by refusing to reveal his assets or submit to a [DNA test](/wiki/DNA_profiling "DNA profiling"). Thereafter, another out-of-court settlement was reached.[[81]](#cite_note-Tribune-82)

By the fall of 1998, Malone accepted his paternity of the Ford twins,[[9]](#cite_note-:1-10) and Kay Malone said publicly that the twins were members of the Malone family.[[83]](#cite_note-Tribune3-84) Since that time, Karl Malone has maintained a relationship with the twins,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] each of whom later played college basketball at his alma mater, Louisiana Tech.[[9]](#cite_note-:1-10) Cheryl Ford went on to a 10 year professional basketball career[[9]](#cite_note-:1-10) with the [Detroit Shock](/wiki/Detroit_Shock "Detroit Shock") of the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association").

Bell has played as an [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") offensive lineman for the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"), [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles"), and [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"). In 2008, *[The Buffalo News](/wiki/The_Buffalo_News "The Buffalo News")* reported that Bell's first meeting with Malone came shortly after Bell graduated from high school. According to Bell, Malone told him then that it was "too late" for them to have a father-son relationship.[[84]](#cite_note-Buffalo-85) However, in 2014, the two repaired their relationship and were in regular contact by 2018.[[85]](#cite_note-Deseret-86)

In 2018, Malone said about his relationship with his older children, "I didn't handle it right; I was wrong. ... Father Time is the biggest thief that's out there, and you can't get that back."[[9]](#cite_note-:1-10)[[85]](#cite_note-Deseret-86)

### Religion

In a 2013 interview, Malone identified as a [Southern Baptist](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention") and his wife and children are [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[86]](#cite_note-espn-homophobia-87) He is the [godfather](/wiki/Godparent#Catholic_Church "Godparent") to the youngest son of [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton").[[87]](#cite_note-malone_godfather-88)

### Politics

Malone donated $4,000 to the [2004 reelection campaign](/wiki/George_W._Bush_2004_presidential_campaign "George W. Bush 2004 presidential campaign") of President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and $2,000 to the [2004 U.S. Senate campaign](/wiki/United_States_Senate_election_in_Alaska,_2004 "United States Senate election in Alaska, 2004") of [Lisa Murkowski](/wiki/Lisa_Murkowski "Lisa Murkowski"), a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") from Alaska. In 2002, Malone donated $1,000 to the [campaign](/wiki/United_States_House_elections,_2002#Utah "United States House elections, 2002") of incumbent U.S. Representative [Jim Matheson](/wiki/Jim_Matheson "Jim Matheson"), a [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") representing [Utah's 2nd congressional district](/wiki/Utah%27s_2nd_congressional_district "Utah's 2nd congressional district").[[88]](#cite_note-89) Malone appeared on the "Great American Panel" segment of the [Fox News](/wiki/Fox_News "Fox News") talk show *[Hannity](/wiki/Hannity "Hannity")* in March 2009. He advocated support for farmers in Louisiana who were about to lose their jobs due to the closing of [Pilgrim's Pride](/wiki/Pilgrim%27s_Pride "Pilgrim's Pride") plants.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Malone has served on the board of directors of the [National Rifle Association of America](/wiki/National_Rifle_Association_of_America "National Rifle Association of America") (NRA), a national firearms advocacy organization.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

### Outdoor life

Malone is an avid hunter and fisherman.[[85]](#cite_note-Deseret-86) He owns a summer home near the [Kenai River](/wiki/Kenai_River "Kenai River") in Alaska.[[91]](#cite_note-92) In July 1998, Malone joined his Jazz teammates [Chris Morris](/wiki/Chris_Morris_(basketball) "Chris Morris (basketball)") and [Bryon Russell](/wiki/Bryon_Russell "Bryon Russell"), some family members, corporate leadership of [Q-Lube](/wiki/Jiffy_Lube "Jiffy Lube"), and winners of a Q-Lube contest for a four-day fishing trip to Alaska.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

### Public image

Malone is known for frequently [referring to himself in the third person](/wiki/Illeism "Illeism").[[93]](#cite_note-94)

He has also appeared in television commercials for LA Gear sneakers,[[94]](#cite_note-95) Starter,[[95]](#cite_note-96) Morris Air Service,[[96]](#cite_note-97) Upper Deck,[[97]](#cite_note-98) and Burger King.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

A cereal dedicated to him, MVP Flakes, was produced in 2000.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

[Vanessa Bryant](/wiki/Vanessa_Bryant "Vanessa Bryant") has accused Malone of multiple incidents of sexual harassment.[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103)
